# A08G Cloud (S26)

## ESE 5160: IoT Edge Computing Deadline: Apr 8, 2026 11:59 PM EDT

| A group submission on Gradescope & GitHub Classroom. Starting from this assignment, you’ll use the same GitHub repository for all remaining assignments related to the final project. Write the answers in the README corresponding to the assignment number.GitHub Classroom Assignment: [https://classroom.github.com/a/1AhllCUs](https://classroom.github.com/a/1AhllCUs)  | ![][image1] |

**Late-day requests:** [https://forms.gle/7hZVrXLZ51XiCExm8](https://forms.gle/7hZVrXLZ51XiCExm8) 

**Course Webpage:** [https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/](https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/)	

Remember to read the [ESE5160 S26 Assignment README](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1DQJxfhTMBN1aE2uasZiKpqR0JOT77pXvb6tUPF7iKF8/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.c5nyj2pvdk6p) before starting\!

**All the required questions are highlighted in green.**

**Important notes are highlighted in yellow.**

# Learning Outcomes

* Learn how to set up a virtual machine to act as our file host, Node-RED host, and MQTT broker.

* Use the virtual machine’s file hosting to complete the over-the-air update capability on the MCU, adding any additional debugging handling.

* Design and implement a compelling and intuitive user interface to control your Internet-connected device.

* Complete the communication loop between the MCU and the cloud using MQTT.

* Learn how to read through given code bases to understand their crucial functionality.

# 

# Errata

This section includes commonly asked questions about the assignment. It will be updated continuously throughout the assignment’s lifecycle, so please check it regularly.

(Currently nothing)

# AI Tools

Accelerated product design cycles require engineering teams to move quickly\! It’s obvious that Artificial Intelligence tools can be immensely helpful in being agile developers. Learning a Hardware Abstraction Layer in great detail and solving minute errors can be time consuming.

While there is no substitute for datasheets, documentation, and debugging, you are encouraged to leverage AI tools in your quest to complete these firmware assignments. However, don’t turn your brain off \- stay curious and don’t accept answers without proper investigation.

# 

# 0\. Starter Manual

Follow the [A08G Cloud Companion S26](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1XiBnE6EMSATGmceByp18nA4BrwboDW4IrxyUXY_yIGA/edit?tab=t.0) to set up your system. This will guide you on how to set up a virtual machine on Microsoft Azure then use this as an HTTP host for firmware binary and status files. It will also show you how to set up a Node-RED instance and an MQTT broker communication server.

# 1\. Over-The-Air Firmware Updates

## Sample Project Introduction

It’s time to begin your final firmware codebase\! For rapid initial prototype development, it’s common to begin from the manufacturer's starter code. Additional features can be added later. In this class, you will base your device code on the **Wi-Fi \- HTTP/HTTPS OTAF Update (SoC)** example.

This firmware project is based on FreeRTOS and shows how communication happens between the Cortex M4 application code and the wireless processing NWP core. HTTPS downloads are supported in this example, with pre-loaded certificates for Azure. This example uses blog storage on your Azure account \- you can choose to use blob storage (likely easiest), or the Apache setup from the [A08G Cloud Companion S26](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1XiBnE6EMSATGmceByp18nA4BrwboDW4IrxyUXY_yIGA/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.14dab8q9tsbb).

An RTOS thread is created called **application\_start**. There is a Wi-Fi connection state machine in this thread \- but where does the OTA magic happen?

| //\! OTAF firmware upgrade status \= sl\_si91x\_http\_otaf\_v2(&http\_params); //\! But what happens inside this function? It’s up to you to research it\! |
| :---- |

Valuable documentation includes: 

*  [Wifi Common Api | Wi-Fi | WiSeConnect | latest | Silicon Labs](https://docs.silabs.com/wiseconnect/latest/wiseconnect-api-reference-guide-wi-fi/wifi-common-api)   
* [Si91x Firmware Update From Module Functions | SiWx91x Device Management | WiSeConnect | latest | Silicon Labs](https://docs.silabs.com/wiseconnect/latest/wiseconnect-api-reference-guide-si91x-driver/si91-x-firmware-update-from-module-functions#sl-si91x-http-otaf) 

| There is a lot to process in this example code. How should you understand what it’s doing? Read the README and the source code\!No better way than to attempt comprehension yourself. It helps if Intellisense is configured correctly in VS Code so that you can CTRL+Click on functions and variables to find their definitions. Run the code using the debugger.This way, you can follow function calls through the HAL, set breakpoints to validate what code is executing, and track variable changes throughout the flow of the program. |
| :---- |

## Sample Project Setup

You can find this using the BRD2708A Rev04 connected (or as a [virtual device](https://docs.silabs.com/ssv6ug/latest/ssv6ug-work-with-devices/02-add-remove-virtual-devices)) and searching for **ota** in the **Example Projects & Demos** tab. Don’t worry \- it’s simple to [re-target the example](https://docs.silabs.com/ssv6ug/latest/ssv6-project-retargeting/) to the **BRD2605A** or the **SIWG917Y121MGABA** module on your custom PCBA.

![][image2]

This example comes with a thorough README.md that details how to set up the project. You’ll need to set up this project to download a firmware binary from Azure cloud storage.

![][image3]

| Note: This example will immediately attempt to connect to a Wi-Fi AP, download, check, and flash the firmware. If you upload this example as the firmware image to download, it will create a perpetual downloading loop by default \- and this may have ramifications for your Azure credits\! |
| :---- |

## Data Security Warning

| Do not commit your Wi-Fi router information to the GitHub repository\! How can you elegantly develop Wi-Fi connected firmware without publishing your private information to a public repo? Use a local-only file with your secrets, but add it to the .gitignore 1\. In the config folder, create a wifi\_secrets.h file. Copy the text below \- it defines the SSID and password for the Wi-Fi network. // wifi\_secrets.h \- DO NOT COMMIT THIS FILE // This will be added to the .gitignore, which won't commit it to the public GitHub repository \#pragma once \#define DEFAULT\_WIFI\_CLIENT\_PROFILE\_SSID "MyRealHomeNetwork" \#define DEFAULT\_WIFI\_CLIENT\_CREDENTIAL   "MySuperSecretPassword" 2\. Update your .gitignore file by adding wifi\_secrets.h to it. 3\. Include wifi\_secrets.h to the header file sl\_net\_default\_values.h from the Azure example, above the inclusion of sl\_net\_wifi\_types.h. \#pragma once \#include "wifi\_secrets.h" // ESE5160: This is where you set your Wi-Fi IDs\! \#include "sl\_net\_wifi\_types.h" 4\. Whenever the repository is downloaded to a new computer, you’ll need to create wifi\_secrets.h in the project \- it’s never committed to GitHub. |
| :---- |

##  

## Sample Project Fixes

Using the SDK 2025.6.3 requires a few changes that are not noted in the README.md.

| In app.c, the included certificate must be updated. | // \#include "azure\_baltimore\_ca.pem.h" \#include "silabs\_dgcert\_ca.pem.h" |
| :---- | :---- |
| AWS is enabled by default in this example. In **app.c**, you must undefine the AWS\_ENABLE macro and define the AZURE\_ENABLE macro. | /\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*                    Constants \*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*/  \#undef AWS\_ENABLE  \#define AZURE\_ENABLE 1 |
| When setting up your Azure file storage, you must enable **Allow Blob anonymous access**. This will allow you to make files public facing so your 917 can view and download the firmware. | ![][image4] |

## 

## Connecting to the Internet at UPenn

Our 917 devices are low power devices without a screen. How could they possibly get connected to a secure Wi-Fi network? [AirPennNet-Device](https://device.upenn.edu/) allows us to get these devices online by leveraging a MAC address registration process.

* You will need your device’s MAC address \- [this function will be helpful](https://docs.silabs.com/wiseconnect/3.5.3/wiseconnect-api-reference-guide-wi-fi/wifi-common-api#sl-wifi-get-mac-address)\!  
  * A MAC address, or **Media Access Control address**, is a unique identifier assigned to all network-capable devices.  
  * An example of what a MAC address looks like is **00:0a:95:9d:68:16.**  
* After you register your device, update your WIFI name & password in the code as below.  
  * **AirPennNet-Device WIFI name**: AirPennNet-Device  
  * **AirPennNet-DevicePassword**: penn1740wifi

**Submission**

* A short video/GIF showing that your device can update its firmware via the Internet. Show both the device behavior and the CLI readout in the video.  
* Code submitted to the GitHub repository.

# 2\. Node-RED Design

Before doing any coding, design the MQTT topics and program flow your system will use. In your **A08G\_README.md**, explaining the following: 

* General program flow of your system. What communication does the device need to send to the cloud? What does the cloud do with this information? What does it send back?  
* List all the topics your system will use. Describe for each one the information that is sent on each topic (is it an integer? A Boolean? An array of integers? A JSON string?)   
* Describe for each topic in your system: Who generates the data? Who subscribes to each topic?  
* Present how you would divide your MCU Application code into threads, how the threads would communicate, etc.

**Submission**

* In the A08G\_README.md section, explain your Node-RED design (the questions above). A reader should be able to understand how your system will work at a high level with this document.  
* Use text and diagrams as needed to explain your approach.

# 

# 3\. Bidirectional Cloud Communication

Now that you have your own VM with Node-RED and MQTT, it’s time to set up communication between your MCU and the cloud. You’ll use your debug LED and debug button with the following behavior:

* Node-RED UI Switch: controls the state of the debug LED on your MCU HW (on or off)  
* MCU Debug Button: turns on a Node-RED UI LED when the HW button is pressed (and turns off the LED when it is not pressed)

Continue using the OTA project from earlier in the assignment. Here are steps you’ll need to take:

1. Disable the firmware update function, but retain the connection to the Wi-Fi access point. We’ll need that to connect to the MQTT broker.  
2. Add in MQTT support through Simplicity Studio.  
   ![][image5]  
3. Add a separate thread for MQTT communication.  
   The API will be very helpful for you: [Service Mqtt Functions | MQTT | WiSeConnect | latest | Silicon Labs](https://docs.silabs.com/wiseconnect/latest/wiseconnect-api-reference-guide-mqtt/service-mqtt-functions)   
   You may find it useful to create a separate MQTT test project, based on the **Wi-Fi \- Embedded MQTT Client (SoC)** example.  
   ![][image6]

**Submission**

* A gif/video of this behavior working.  
* Create a Node-RED folder in your repo and commit your Node-RED source code to the folder.  
* Submit a link to your Node-RED UI (the public URL).  
* Commit your firmware to the Github repository.

# 4\. Software Architecture

Before diving into this assignment, take a moment to consider the overall software architecture of your project, as discussed in[L10-S26 Embedded I - Concurrency.pdf](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1iusxFFioZHdC0ODiY_6gLktPM4IiYRZ0/view?usp=drive_link). Understanding how components interact will help you design a more robust and scalable system.

**Submission:**

1. Revisit your Hardware and Software Requirements Specification (HRS & SRS) in **A00G**.  
   Update the requirements based on the latest modifications to your project, and place the updated HRS & SRS in **A08G\_README.md**.

2. Submit a block diagram outlining the different tasks into which you are dividing your software; below is an example:  
   ![][image7]

3. Submit flowcharts or state machine diagrams briefly illustrating how each task operates; go to [L10-S26 Embedded I - Concurrency.pdf](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1iusxFFioZHdC0ODiY_6gLktPM4IiYRZ0/view?usp=drive_link) for an example.

# Rubric

See the Gradescope assignment for the rubric details. For all questions, 0 points may be awarded if the submission is non-existent, very poorly done, or doesn’t compile (for firmware assignments).
